  • Portal Particle Acceleration In Jets

Recollimation shocks

Recollimation shocks

Type:Research
Educational level:Research
Key Papers:Bromberg et al. (2011), Peretti et al. (2603.16647)

Recollimation shocks occur when an expanding jet is squeezed by external pressure (for example, from a cocoon) causing the flow to converge and form a standing shock. These shocks are often oblique to the jet axis and can be quasi-stationary over long times.

Physical description

  • Geometry — The shock surface is typically conical or parabolic; obliquity (angle with respect to flow) controls compression and downstream pressure.
  • Strength — Even highly inclined recollimation shocks can be strong if the upstream Mach number is high and the cocoon pressure is sufficient.
  • DSA at recollimation shocks — Despite obliquity, DSA can operate efficiently if turbulence scatters particles across the shock; confinement and diffusion regime set E_max.

Why this matters

Peretti et al. (2603.16647) show that recollimation shocks in sub-relativistic jets are viable cosmic-ray accelerators, predicting source-class-dependent maximum energies (protostars → TeV; microquasars → tens of PeV; Seyferts → PeV–EeV under optimistic turbulence). Understanding recollimation shocks links jet dynamics to high-energy signatures in cocoons and surrounding media.

Key references

  1. Bromberg et al. 2011 — analytic jet–cocoon framework used to locate recollimation shocks.
  2. Peretti et al. 2603.16647 — study of DSA at recollimation shocks in sub-relativistic jets.

Related concepts

  • Jet–Cocoon systems
  • Diffusive shock acceleration
  • Particle acceleration — oblique shocks

See also

  • Peretti et al. (2603.16647)
